input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-13T15:08:58.997Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user has migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is encountering exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their test project to understand the setup and provide the fix.

data.content:
Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns **exit code 8** when zero tests are discovered — unlike VSTest which returned 0. Since your tests are conditionally compiled away in some configurations, you get this "no tests found" failure.

The fix is to set the `TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure` property to `false` in your `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat 'no tests discovered' as success (exit code 0 instead of 8) -->
    <TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>false</TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>

<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat 'no tests discovered' as success (exit code 0 instead of 8) -->
    <TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>false</TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>

    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>", "new_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat 'no tests discovered' as success (exit code 0 instead of 8) -->
    <TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>false</TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>"}

data.content: The property didn't resolve it. The correct fix for Microsoft.Testing.Platform is to set `--minimum-expected-tests 0`. Let me apply the proper solution:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow zero tests without failing (matches old VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.old_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat 'no tests discovered' as success (exit code 0 instead of 8) -->
    <TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>false</TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure>

data.reasoningText:
The `TestingPlatformShowTestsFailure` property didn't seem to work. Let me check what the correct property name is. The behavior in Microsoft.Testing.Platform is controlled by a different setting. Let me look into this more carefully.

Actually, looking at this more carefully, the correct MSBuild property for Microsoft.Testing.Platform to suppress the "no tests" exit code might be different. Let me research this.

In Microsoft.Testing.Platform, exit code 8 means "no tests found". The way to suppress this is through a command-line option or configuration. Let me check what the correct approach is.

Actually, I think the correct property might be:


The exit code 8 in Microsoft.Testing.Platform indicates "minimum expected tests not met", which defaults to 1. I can set it to 0 using the `--minimum-expected-tests 0` command-line argument, or configure it in the project file through `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments`. I need to look up what command-line options are available for this testing platform.

data.reasoningText:
Hmm, that's still not working. Let me check if the command line argument is being passed correctly. Let me try running the test executable directly or look at what options are available.

Let me check the test results log and also try a different approach.

data.content: The log reveals `--minimum-expected-tests` doesn't accept 0. But there's a `--ignore-exit-code` option designed exactly for this. Exit code 8 = "no tests found". Let me use that:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Exit code 8 = no tests found; treat as success to match VSTest behavior -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.old_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow zero tests without failing (matches old VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
I can see the issue. The `--minimum-expected-tests` option requires a "non-zero positive integer value". So we can't set it to 0. However, I notice there's another option: `--ignore-exit-code` which says "Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)".

This is perfect! Exit code 8 is the "no tests discovered" exit code, and we can use `--ignore-exit-code 8` to suppress it.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Exit code 8 = no tests found; treat as success to match VSTest behavior -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.old_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Allow zero tests without failing (matches old VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.content:
**Fix:** Add this property to your `.csproj`:

```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

**What happened:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code 8 when no tests are discovered (unlike VSTest which returned 0). The `--ignore-exit-code 8` flag tells the runner to map that specific exit code back to 0, restoring VSTest behavior for empty test assemblies while still failing on actual test failures.
